title: Chapter 11: Office Hours at the End of the World
content: Volume Two: The Kingdom Expands
<!-- Speed: Sprint | D3+D7 -->
The Santa Monica Pier became Chloe's throne room.

Not officially. There was no sign, no permit, no "QUEEN ARTEMIS — CONSULTATIONS" banner. Just a bench near the carousel, a foam sword leaning against the railing, and a growing number of people who had driven hours — sometimes from other states — to sit next to a woman in a plastic crown and have her explain their lives back to them.
Sam kept the sign-up sheet. It was a Google Form, because of course it was. The questions included:

1. Name
2. What's actually wrong? (Be honest. No one else will see this.)
3. When did you last call your mother?
4. On a scale of 1-10, how much of your personality is borrowed from people you follow online?
Question four filtered out a surprising number of applicants.

The first consultation of Volume Two was a man named Marcus. He drove from San Diego. He was thirty-four, worked in tech, and had been told by his therapist that he needed to "find meaning." He had interpreted this as "buy a Peloton."
"Marcus," Chloe said, studying him the way a mechanic studies an engine that's making a noise no one else can hear. "You don't need a Peloton. You need to admit that you hate your job."
"I don't hate my job."

"You drive two hours each way. You've taken three mental health days this month. Your lunch is a Soylent you drink at your desk while reading Hacker News, which you also hate. Your therapist told you to find meaning, and you interpreted that as 'acquire an expensive stationary bicycle.'"
Marcus opened his mouth. Closed it.

"The bicycle is not the problem," Chloe continued. "The problem is that you've been optimizing a life you don't want. You're a Level 47 Software Engineer with zero points in Self-Knowledge. You min-maxed the wrong stats."
"I... I don't play RPGs."
"That's also part of the problem."
Sam, sitting at the next bench with her laptop open, added a note to the spreadsheet: *Marcus — Level 47 Engineer, INT 18, WIS 4. Prescription: quit job, play D&D, call mother (3 years).*
Marcus looked at the ocean. The ocean, as always, had no opinion about his career choices.
"What do I do?" he asked quietly.
"You know what to do," Chloe said. "You knew before you got in the car to drive here. You just needed someone in a plastic crown to tell you it was okay."
Marcus sat in silence. Then he laughed — a short, startled laugh, the kind people make when they realize the thing they've been terrified of admitting is actually the thing that will save them.
"I'm going to quit my job," he said.
"Obviously."
"And call my mom."
"Grass is free."
Marcus stood up. He looked at Chloe. Then at Sam. Then at the spreadsheet. "Is there a fee for this?"
"Free," Chloe said.
"But how do you—"
"We don't. That's the point."

Marcus walked away toward the parking lot. His posture had changed — shoulders back, head up. He looked like a man who had just canceled a Peloton subscription and was about to cancel several other things.
"Soul number eight," Sam said, updating the spreadsheet.
"The kingdom grows."
That was when the problem arrived.

Her name was Celeste. She had 2 million TikTok followers, a merchandise line, and a plastic crown that was noticeably more expensive than Chloe's — rhinestones, actual metal accents, probably cost more than Shadowfax's new transmission.
She was standing at the edge of the pier, flanked by a cameraman and a person holding a ring light, wearing an embroidered poncho that said "QUEEN CELESTE" on the back.
"Oh no," Sam said.
"What?"
"Someone's monetized your bit."
Chloe turned. She saw the rhinestone crown. The branded poncho. The ring light. The cameraman.
"A pretender to the throne," Chloe said quietly.
"A content creator who saw your viral moment and built a business model around it," Sam corrected.
"Same thing."
Celeste spotted Chloe and her trajectory changed — the way a heat-seeking missile adjusts course. She glided over, cameraman in tow, ring light glowing like a second sun.
"Oh my god!" Celeste's voice was pitched somewhere between genuine excitement and the algorithm. "You're the original! The strawberry farm girl! Can we get a collab?"
"A what?"
"A collaboration! Queen Celeste meets Queen Artemis. Two queens, one pier. My audience would lose their minds."

Chloe looked at Celeste's crown. The rhinestones. The branded poncho. The cameraman. The ring light. The merchandise line — she was pretty sure there was a link in Celeste's bio for $45 "Queen Energy" candles.
"You're selling candles," Chloe said.
"They're soy-based!"
"You're selling enlightenment-themed soy candles and calling yourself a queen."
"I'm spreading your message!" Celeste said brightly. "Authenticity. Liberation. Main character energy. I just... packaged it."
"You commodified it."
Celeste's smile flickered — the same flicker Chloe had seen on Zenith, on Madison, on Greg. The flicker of someone whose business model had just been named out loud.
"I'm building a brand," Celeste said, less brightly.
"You're building a souvenir shop on top of something that's supposed to be free."
Sam, from the bench, said quietly: "Called it."
Celeste's cameraman was still filming. The ring light was still glowing. Somewhere, content was being created.

"You know what your problem is?" Celeste said, dropping the bright voice entirely. "You could be making six figures. You could have a brand. A line. A podcast. Instead you're sitting on a bench eating burritos and giving free advice to strangers."
"Yes," Chloe said.
"That's not a business model."
"It's not supposed to be."
"Then what is it?"
Chloe considered the question. The ocean crashed below them. The carousel played its tinny, ancient music. The seagulls had opinions about everything.
"It's a life," Chloe said. "I already had a business model. It made me have a breakdown in a Zoom meeting. So I'm trying something else."
Celeste stared at her. The ring light flickered — battery dying, or maybe just the universe offering a metaphor.
"That's not scalable," Celeste said finally.
"Neither is happiness."
Celeste opened her mouth. Nothing came out. She looked at her cameraman. The cameraman shrugged — the shrug of someone who was paid hourly and had no opinion about the ontology of joy.
"Come on," Celeste said to her crew. "We're finding a different pier."
They left. The ring light dimmed. The branded poncho retreated toward the parking lot.
"Pretender defeated," Sam said.
"That wasn't a defeat. That was a diagnosis."
"Same thing."
